paragraphs.text: sc.version

%pyspark
from pyspark.sql import functions as F
from pyspark.sql.types import FloatType, IntegerType

%pyspark
raw_df = spark.read.csv("hdfs:///user/maria_dev/healthcare/health.csv", header=True, inferSchema=True)

%pyspark
# Standardize column names (Clean '2000 [YR2000]' -> '2000')
cleaned_cols = []
for col_name in raw_df.columns:
    if "[YR" in col_name:
        clean_name = col_name.split(" ")[0]
        # Using .format() instead of f-strings for older Python compatibility
        # Notice the use of backticks ` inside the quotes!
        cleaned_cols.append(F.col("`{}`".format(col_name)).alias(clean_name))
    else:
        cleaned_cols.append(F.col("`{}`".format(col_name)))

standardized_df = raw_df.select(cleaned_cols)

# Replace World Bank string nulls ('..') with native Spark Nulls
year_columns = [str(year) for year in range(2000, 2025)]
for year_col in year_columns:
    standardized_df = standardized_df.withColumn(
        year_col, 
        F.when(F.col(year_col) == "..", None).otherwise(F.col(year_col).cast(FloatType()))
    )

print("Raw Data Ingested and Schema Standardized:")
standardized_df.printSchema()

paragraphs.text:
%md
Reshaping & Normalization

%pyspark

# Unpivot (Melt) years from wide columns to long rows using .format()
stack_expr = ", ".join(["'{}', `{}`".format(y, y) for y in year_columns])
melt_expression = "stack({}, {}) as (Year, Value)".format(len(year_columns), stack_expr)

long_df = standardized_df.select(
    F.col("Country Name").alias("country_name"),
    F.col("Country Code").alias("country_code"),
    F.col("Series Code").alias("series_code"),
    F.expr(melt_expression)
).filter(F.col("Value").isNotNull())

# Pivot Series Codes into separate indicator columns
reshaped_df = long_df.groupBy("country_name", "country_code", "Year") \
    .pivot("series_code") \
    .agg(F.first("Value"))

# Cast columns and simplify names for clean analytical processing
final_structured_df = reshaped_df \
    .withColumn("Year", F.col("Year").cast(IntegerType())) \
    .withColumnRenamed("SH.XPD.CHEX.GD.ZS", "health_expenditure_gdp") \
    .withColumnRenamed("SH.XPD.GHED.PC.CD", "health_expenditure_per_capita") \
    .withColumnRenamed("SH.MED.BEDS.ZS", "hospital_beds_per_1k") \
    .withColumnRenamed("SP.DYN.LE00.IN", "life_expectancy") \
    .withColumnRenamed("SP.DYN.IMRT.IN", "infant_mortality") \
    .withColumnRenamed("SP.POP.65UP.TO.ZS", "population_above_65_pct")

print("Data Reshaped into Analytical Schema:")
final_structured_df.printSchema()

msg.data:
Data Reshaped into Analytical Schema:
root
 |-- country_name: string (nullable = true)
 |-- country_code: string (nullable = true)
 |-- Year: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- hospital_beds_per_1k: float (nullable = true)
 |-- health_expenditure_gdp: float (nullable = true)
 |-- health_expenditure_per_capita: float (nullable = true)
 |-- infant_mortality: float (nullable = true)
 |-- life_expectancy: float (nullable = true)
 |-- population_above_65_pct: float (nullable = true)

paragraphs.text:
%md
Data Cleaning

%pyspark
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

# Define a analytical window partitioned strictly by country
country_window = Window.partitionBy("country_code")

# Calculate historical country averages for the sparse metric
country_mean_beds = F.avg("hospital_beds_per_1k").over(country_window)

# Impute missing values conditionally
cleaned_pipeline_df = final_structured_df.withColumn(
    "hospital_beds_per_1k",
    F.coalesce(F.col("hospital_beds_per_1k"), country_mean_beds)
)

# Cache the cleaned dataset in cluster memory for high-performance retrieval
cleaned_pipeline_df.cache()

# Using .format() for the print statement
print("Data Cleaning Completed. Total clean records: {}".format(cleaned_pipeline_df.count()))

msg.data: Data Cleaning Completed. Total clean records: 200

paragraphs.text:
%md
Visualisations

%pyspark

# Securely persist the dataset into the Apache Hive Warehouse
try:
    # This physically writes the table into the cluster's Hadoop/Hive ecosystem
    cleaned_pipeline_df.write \
        .mode("overwrite") \
        .saveAsTable("global_health_metrics")
    print("Success: Data successfully persisted to Apache Hive table 'global_health_metrics'.")
except Exception as e:
    print("Warehouse storage exception: {}. Falling back to in-memory view.".format(str(e)))
    cleaned_pipeline_df.createOrReplaceTempView("global_health_metrics")

# Expose the data view so your SQL paragraphs don't break
cleaned_pipeline_df.createOrReplaceTempView("global_health_metrics")
print("Data view 'global_health_metrics' is ready for visual reporting!")

msg.data:
Success: Data successfully persisted to Apache Hive table 'global_health_metrics'.
Data view 'global_health_metrics' is ready for visual reporting!

paragraphs.text:
%md
## Analysis of mortality rate by country

* Indonesia has the highest average infant mortality rate at 26.1 per 1000 live births. However, Indonesia also spends the least on healthcare, at 2.71% of their GDP while having the second highest net life expectancy growth of 5.8 years.
* Singapore has the least infant mortality rate, at 2.26, beating Japan which has an infant mortality rate of 2.32. This highlights Singapore's efficient healthcare system.

msg.data:
Analysis of mortality rate by country

  • Indonesia has the highest average infant mortality rate at 26.1 per 1000 live births. However, Indonesia also spends the least on healthcare, at 2.71% of their GDP while having the second highest net life expectancy growth of 5.8 years.
  • Singapore has the least infant mortality rate, at 2.26, beating Japan which has an infant mortality rate of 2.32. This highlights Singapore's efficient healthcare system.

paragraphs.text:
%sql
-- Annual percentage growth rate per capita of healthcare spending
-- Use THA because has best progress (regional peer benchmark), EAS because regional benchmark (middle ground)

WITH SpendingTrends AS (
    SELECT 
        Year,
        country_name,
        country_code,
        health_expenditure_per_capita AS current_spend,
        LAG(health_expenditure_per_capita, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY country_name ORDER BY Year) AS previous_spend
    FROM 
        global_health_metrics
)
SELECT 
    Year,
    country_name AS Country,
    ROUND(current_spend, 2) AS Spend_Per_Capita_USD,
    ROUND(((current_spend - previous_spend) / previous_spend) * 100, 2) AS YoY_Growth_Rate_Pct
FROM 
    SpendingTrends
WHERE 
    previous_spend IS NOT NULL 
    AND Year >= 2002
    -- Native Spark SQL array checking: clean, robust, and handles spaces perfectly
    AND array_contains(split('${checkbox:Select Countries=MYS,MYS|SGP|IDN|THA|VNM|JPN|USA|EAS}', ','), country_code)
ORDER BY 
    Country, Year ASC

paragraphs.text:
%md
## Analysis of plot of annual percentage growth rate per capita of healthcare spending
* Uses LAG function to compute the exact annual percentage growth rate of per capita healthcare spending. It shows which nation is aggressively spending more on healthcare systems.
* Malaysia is compared to a peer benchmark (Thailand) and a regional benchmark (East and Pacific Asia)
* Malaysia has the higest spending in 2006 (36.23), way above the regional benchmark, and lowest spending in 2022 (-13.54).
* in 2021, Malaysia's spending is higher due to the pandemic. Once the pandemic starts to subside, around 2022, funding can be reduced, which is why there is a huge negative spike in 2022.

msg.data:
Analysis of plot of annual percentage growth rate per capita of healthcare spending

  • Uses LAG function to compute the exact annual percentage growth rate of per capita healthcare spending. It shows which nation is aggressively spending more on healthcare systems.
  • Malaysia is compared to a peer benchmark (Thailand) and a regional benchmark (East and Pacific Asia)
  • Malaysia has the higest spending in 2006 (36.23), way above the regional benchmark, and lowest spending in 2022 (-13.54).
  • in 2021, Malaysia's spending is higher due to the pandemic. Once the pandemic starts to subside, around 2022, funding can be reduced, which is why there is a huge negative spike in 2022.

paragraphs.text:
%sql
-- Benchmark Malaysia vs regional baseline (east asia and pacific)
WITH RegionalBaseline AS (
    SELECT 
        Year, 
        life_expectancy AS regional_avg 
    FROM 
        global_health_metrics 
    WHERE 
        country_code = 'EAS'
)
SELECT 
    m.Year,
    ROUND(m.life_expectancy, 1) AS Malaysia_Life_Exp,
    ROUND(r.regional_avg, 1) AS Regional_Avg_Life_Exp,
    ROUND(m.life_expectancy - r.regional_avg, 2) AS Malaysia_Outperformance_Years
FROM 
    global_health_metrics m
JOIN 
    RegionalBaseline r ON m.Year = r.Year
WHERE 
    m.country_code = 'MYS'
ORDER BY 
    m.Year ASC

%md
## Analysis of plot of Malaysia's performance against regional benchmark (East and Pacific Asia)
* Isolates Malaysia and uses a self-join to compute the gap between Malaysia's life expectancy and East Asia and Pacific Asia baseline for each year.
* Positive bars are observed from 2000 until 2013, meaning Malaysia's performance is ahead of the regional benchmark.
* Negative bars are observed from 2014 until 2022, meaning Malaysia's life expectancy is lagging behind the regional benchmark.
* Huge negative spike in 2021, which is the period of the COVID-19 pandemic where life expectancy is shorter.
* Increase in life expectancy starting from 2023 until 2024.

msg.data:
Analysis of plot of Malaysia's performance against regional benchmark (East and Pacific Asia)

  • Isolates Malaysia and uses a self-join to compute the gap between Malaysia's life expectancy and East Asia and Pacific Asia baseline for each year.
  • Positive bars are observed from 2000 until 2013, meaning Malaysia's performance is ahead of the regional benchmark.
  • Negative bars are observed from 2014 until 2022, meaning Malaysia's life expectancy is lagging behind the regional benchmark.
  • Huge negative spike in 2021, which is the period of the COVID-19 pandemic where life expectancy is shorter.
  • Increase in life expectancy starting from 2023 until 2024.

paragraphs.text:
%sql

SELECT 
    country_name AS Country,
    ROUND(AVG(health_expenditure_gdp), 2) AS Avg_Healthcare_Spending_GDP_Pct,
    ROUND(AVG(life_expectancy), 1) AS Avg_Life_Expectancy_Years,
    ROUND(AVG(infant_mortality), 1) AS Avg_Infant_Mortality
FROM 
    global_health_metrics
WHERE 
    country_code != 'EAS'  -- Crucial filter: removes the macro aggregate to keep comparisons strictly peer-to-peer
GROUP BY 
    country_name
ORDER BY 
    Avg_Life_Expectancy_Years DESC

msg.data:
Country	Avg_Healthcare_Spending_GDP_Pct	Avg_Life_Expectancy_Years	Avg_Infant_Mortality
Japan	9.53	83.1	2.3
Singapore	3.76	81.5	2.3
United States	15.7	78.0	6.1
Malaysia	3.38	75.0	6.7
Thailand	3.7	74.9	11.8
Viet Nam	4.58	73.8	16.4
Indonesia	2.71	68.6	26.1

paragraphs.text:
%sql

SELECT 
    Year,
    country_name,
    health_expenditure_gdp,
    life_expectancy,
    hospital_beds_per_1k
FROM 
    global_health_metrics
WHERE 
    country_code = '${country=MYS}'
ORDER BY 
    Year ASC

%md
## Analysis of Results

The outputs from the global health metrics query reveal several critical insights regarding the relationship between healthcare expenditure and population health outcomes:

* Higher spending does not necessarily guarantee better health outcomes. For example, the United States spends 15.7% of their GDP on healthcare but the average life expectancy is only 78.0 years while having a growth of only 2.6 years.
* Japan has the highest average life expectancy of 83.1 years while maintaining a moderate spending of 9.53% of their GDP. Singapore has the second highest average life expectancy at 81.5 years, while spending only 3.76% of their GDP and also having a larger life expectancy growth of 5.6. Both of these nations have efficient infrastructure resource allocation.
* Malaysia has a highly convservative spending of 3.38% of their GDP while maintaining a moderate average life expectancy of 75.0 years. However, Thailand, which has a similar spending and average life expectancy, is able to achieve a greater net life expectancy growth over Malaysia at 6.4 years.
* Malaysia’s infrastructure trends shows a steady, reduction on facilities, with local hospital beds per 1,000 citizens declining from 2.05 in the year 2000 down to approximately 1.92 by the year 2024.


## Recommendations

Drawing from the comparative analysis of these health indicators, the following actionable recommendations are proposed for policymakers:

* Implementing an efficient healthcare system provides the best outcome for human health, as validated in the results by nations like Singapore. Although a country may spend a large portion of its GDP on healthcare, the returns are diminishing if the underlying framework is not sufficient or flawed.
* Thailand is a notable nation, in the sense that they achieved a 6.4 year life expectancy growth with a relatively low percentage of GDP spending. Developing nations should look to Thailand's healthcare policies to identify reproducible strategies.
* Life expectancy in Malaysia is steadily rising. However, there is a decline in the number of hospital beds per 1000 citizens, indicating that better, more strategic investment is needed. As the population ages, unresolved infrastructure issues will heavily impact long-term healthcare systems.

msg.data:
Analysis of Results

The outputs from the global health metrics query reveal several critical insights regarding the relationship between healthcare expenditure and population health outcomes:

  • Higher spending does not necessarily guarantee better health outcomes. For example, the United States spends 15.7% of their GDP on healthcare but the average life expectancy is only 78.0 years while having a growth of only 2.6 years.
  • Japan has the highest average life expectancy of 83.1 years while maintaining a moderate spending of 9.53% of their GDP. Singapore has the second highest average life expectancy at 81.5 years, while spending only 3.76% of their GDP and also having a larger life expectancy growth of 5.6. Both of these nations have efficient infrastructure resource allocation.
  • Malaysia has a highly convservative spending of 3.38% of their GDP while maintaining a moderate average life expectancy of 75.0 years. However, Thailand, which has a similar spending and average life expectancy, is able to achieve a greater net life expectancy growth over Malaysia at 6.4 years.
  • Malaysia’s infrastructure trends shows a steady, reduction on facilities, with local hospital beds per 1,000 citizens declining from 2.05 in the year 2000 down to approximately 1.92 by the year 2024.

Recommendations

Drawing from the comparative analysis of these health indicators, the following actionable recommendations are proposed for policymakers:

  • Implementing an efficient healthcare system provides the best outcome for human health, as validated in the results by nations like Singapore. Although a country may spend a large portion of its GDP on healthcare, the returns are diminishing if the underlying framework is not sufficient or flawed.
  • Thailand is a notable nation, in the sense that they achieved a 6.4 year life expectancy growth with a relatively low percentage of GDP spending. Developing nations should look to Thailand's healthcare policies to identify reproducible strategies.
  • Life expectancy in Malaysia is steadily rising. However, there is a decline in the number of hospital beds per 1000 citizens, indicating that better, more strategic investment is needed. As the population ages, unresolved infrastructure issues will heavily impact long-term healthcare systems.

paragraphs.text:
%md
## Conclusion

This data engineering and analytics pipeline successfully ingested, standardized, and analyzed a complex, sparse global healthcare dataset. By using tools like PySpark for distributed data processing and Spark SQL for analytical querying, the raw, unformatted records are able to be transformed into an aggregated, analytical schema.

The following exploratory data analysis highlighted that while financial investment in healthcare is necessary, the *efficiency* of that investment is the true driver of extended life expectancy. The findings underline the importance of data-driven policy-making, demonstrating that developing nations can achieve world-class health outcomes through targeted, highly efficient public health strategies rather than strictly mirroring the heavy expenditure models of Western economies.

msg.data:
Conclusion

This data engineering and analytics pipeline successfully ingested, standardized, and analyzed a complex, sparse global healthcare dataset. By using tools like PySpark for distributed data processing and Spark SQL for analytical querying, the raw, unformatted records are able to be transformed into an aggregated, analytical schema.

The following exploratory data analysis highlighted that while financial investment in healthcare is necessary, the efficiency of that investment is the true driver of extended life expectancy. The findings underline the importance of data-driven policy-making, demonstrating that developing nations can achieve world-class health outcomes through targeted, highly efficient public health strategies rather than strictly mirroring the heavy expenditure models of Western economies.
